title: Remove Two Letters
description: Dmitry has a string $$$s$$$, consisting of lowercase Latin letters. Dmitry decided to remove two consecutive characters from the string $$$s$$$ and you are wondering how many different strings can be obtained after such an operation. For example, Dmitry has a string " aaabcc ". You can get the following different strings: " abcc "(by deleting the first two or second and third characters), " aacc "(by deleting the third and fourth characters)," aaac "(by deleting the fourth and the fifth character) and " aaab " (by deleting the last two).
input_spec: The first line of input data contains a single integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$) — number of test cases. The descriptions of the test cases follow. The first line of the description of each test case contains an integer $$$n$$$ ($$$3 \le n \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$). The second line of the description of each test case contains a string $$$s$$$ of length $$$n$$$ consisting of lowercase Latin letters. It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ for all test cases does not exceed $$$2 \cdot 10^5$$$.
output_spec: For each test case print one integer — the number of distinct strings that can be obtained by removing two consecutive letters.

routes.name: Adjacent-duplicate counting
routes.description: Enumerate all n-1 removal positions, recognize that only adjacent positions can produce duplicates, characterize exactly when two adjacent removals collide (s[i] == s[i+2]), and subtract the collision count from n-1.

observations.title: There are exactly n-1 candidate removal positions
observations.description: Removing two consecutive characters starting at index i (0-indexed) is possible for i = 0, 1, ..., n-2. This gives exactly n-1 candidate operations, each producing a string of length n-2.

hints.nudge: How many ways can you choose a pair of consecutive characters in a string of length n?
hints.question: If the string has length n, what are the valid starting indices for removing two consecutive characters? How many such indices exist?
hints.formal: The starting index i ranges from 0 to n-2, giving n-1 total removal positions. Each produces a string s[0..i-1] + s[i+2..n-1] of length n-2.

observations.title: Only adjacent removal positions can produce duplicates
observations.description: Removing at position i gives s[0..i-1] + s[i+2..n-1]. If two removal positions differ by 2 or more, the resulting strings differ in length of the shared prefix or suffix and cannot be equal. Only positions i and i+1 can possibly yield the same result.

hints.nudge: Compare the results of removing at two positions that are far apart. Do the resulting strings look the same?
hints.question: If you remove at position i, the prefix is s[0..i-1]. If you remove at position j where j >= i+2, what is the prefix? Can these two strings ever be identical character-by-character?
hints.formal: Removing at position i produces prefix s[0..i-1] and removing at position j > i+1 produces prefix s[0..j-1]. Since j-1 >= i+1, the prefixes have different lengths, so the resulting strings differ at index i where one has s[i+2] and the other has s[i]. For non-adjacent positions, these necessarily differ.

observations.title: Two adjacent removals collide iff s[i] == s[i+2]
observations.description: Removing at position i gives s[0..i-1] + s[i+2..n-1]. Removing at position i+1 gives s[0..i] + s[i+3..n-1]. These two strings share the prefix s[0..i-1] and the suffix s[i+3..n-1]. They differ only at index i of the result: the first has s[i+2] there and the second has s[i]. So they are equal if and only if s[i] == s[i+2].

hints.nudge: Write out the two resulting strings side by side. Which character position is the only one that could differ?
hints.question: When you remove at position i, the character at index i in the result is s[i+2]. When you remove at position i+1, what character sits at that same index? When are they equal?
hints.formal: Result of removing at i: s[0], ..., s[i-1], s[i+2], s[i+3], ..., s[n-1]. Result of removing at i+1: s[0], ..., s[i-1], s[i], s[i+3], ..., s[n-1]. These agree everywhere except at index i, where the first has s[i+2] and the second has s[i]. Equal iff s[i] == s[i+2].

observations.title: Answer is (n-1) minus the number of collisions
observations.description: Start with n-1 candidate strings. Each pair of adjacent positions where s[i] == s[i+2] produces a duplicate, reducing the distinct count by one. Count these collisions for i = 0, 1, ..., n-3 and subtract from n-1.

hints.nudge: You know the total number of candidates and which pairs produce duplicates. How do you combine these?
hints.question: If there are n-1 removal positions and you find that k pairs of adjacent positions produce the same string, how many distinct strings are there?
hints.formal: Let collisions = |{i : 0 <= i <= n-3 and s[i] == s[i+2]}|. The answer is (n-1) - collisions. This runs in O(n) time with a single pass through the string.
